title: Container log file is not exist
body:
### Issue Description

I'm running a podman container in detached and rootless mode as follows:
```
  podman run -d \
    -v /podman/models-files:/root/models-files \
    -v /podman/api-tmp:/tmp \
    -v /podman/api-libs:/usr/local/lib \
    --name "${CONTAINER_NAME}" \
    -p 8000:8000 \
    localhost/api:latest
```

when I do `podman logs -f ${CONTAINER_NAME}` it's perfectly fine and I can see my container logs but I need these logs so I'll start monitoring and collecting errors from these logs. 

I checked following locations:
- `/home/<user>/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/<containerID>/userdata/` -> there is no `ctr.log`
- `/var/lib/containers/storage` -> not even exist since I didn't run any container in privileged mode

I also search whole server for `ctr.log`:
`find /* -name "ctr.log" -type f`

There is not results. It's not only for this container, all other containers don't seem to have log files anywhere in the server. They are running with  `-d` which is `detached` so I'm sure it's not the problem.

I also tried:
1. `podman logs -f <containerID>`
2. Then `ps aux | grep podman`
3. `pgrep -P <podman logs command pid>`
4. So I'd see if it uses some OS binaries to tail logs then I'd discover the logs path but no, it does not use any OS binary to do that and it does not reveal the path anyway. 

I'm wondering if this version of the podman is keeping logs with a different name, or keeping them in memory, or it's just a bug? 

We had other machines that was running in same version but log files was getting created.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create a container in detached and rootless mode
2. Check if `ctr.log` is created


### Describe the results you received

`ctr.log` is not created in the  `/home/<user>/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/<containerID>/userdata/`

### Describe the results you expected

`ctr.log` to be created in the  `/home/<user>/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/<containerID>/userdata/`

### podman info output


### Additional information

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @Gnyblast.

The `podman info` output shows `logDriver: journald` which means that `journald` is used a backend for the container logs.  Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-create.1.html#log-driver-driver) for other log drivers.

The description above suggests that you're attempting to read the logs from disk and podman-internal paths (e.g., `/home/<user>/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/<containerID>/userdata/ `).  Those _internal_ structures may not change in the foreseeable future but they are not supported and may change at some point.  It's better to rely on the output of `podman logs` or even using `journalctl` when using the `journald` driver.

After I made the ticket and went over the `podman info` I saw that it uses `journald` as `logDriver`. My local uses `k8s-file` as `logDriver` and it creates `ctr.log` I think I need to switch the server to use `k8s-file` as `logDriver` as well.

> Thanks for reaching out, @Gnyblast.
> 
> The `podman info` output shows `logDriver: journald` which means that `journald` is used a backend for the container logs. Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-create.1.html#log-driver-driver) for other log drivers.
> 
> The description above suggests that you're attempting to read the logs from disk and podman-internal paths (e.g., `/home/<user>/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay-containers/<containerID>/userdata/ `). Those _internal_ structures may not change in the foreseeable future but they are not supported and may change in the future. It's better to rely on the output of `podman logs` or even using `journalctl` when using the `journald` driver.

Yeah exactly the `journald` is the problem for me. Thanks.
You can use containers.conf to set these settings globally on the host.  The `man containers.conf` man page explains how to do that but feel free to reach out here if you any question.
Thanks, I'm on it atm. I imagine I can also create `~/.config/containers/containers.conf` for local users right?

> Thanks, I'm on it atm. I imagine I can also create `~/.config/containers.conf` for local users right?

`~/.config/containers/containers.conf` will work :+1:
